title: 29  Matplotlib - subplots
text:
The subplot function allows you to create multiple plots within a single window or figure. This makes it possible to compare different plots that share a common context, or to present different aspects of the data.
The function signature is plt.subplot(nrows, ncols, index, **kwargs), where:

nrows - the number of rows in the plot grid.
ncols - the number of columns in the plot grid.
index - the index of the current plot to be created (indexing starts at 1). Indices are numbered row by row, i.e. the next plot in a row will have an index one greater.
**kwargs - additional arguments related to plot formatting.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)

# Creating a 2x2 grid of plots
# First plot (top-left corner)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 1)
plt.plot(x, np.sin(x))
plt.title('sin(x)')

# Second plot (top-right corner)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 2)
plt.plot(x, np.cos(x))
plt.title('cos(x)')

# Third plot (bottom-left corner)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 3)
plt.plot(x, np.tan(x))
plt.title('tan(x)')

# Fourth plot (bottom-right corner)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 4)
plt.plot(x, -np.sin(x))
plt.title('-sin(x)')

# Adjusting the spacing between plots
plt.tight_layout()

# Displaying the plots
plt.show()

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Preparing data for visualization
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)  # Create 100 points in the range from 0 to 10
y1 = np.sin(x)               # Sine function
y2 = np.cos(x)               # Cosine function
y3 = np.exp(-0.2*x) * np.sin(x)  # Damped sine function
y4 = x**2 / 20               # Quadratic function

# Creating the figure and subplots (2 rows, 2 columns)
# In the procedural approach we use pyplot directly
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))  # Set the size of the entire figure (width, height in inches)

# First subplot (top-left)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 1)  # 2 rows, 2 columns, position 1
plt.plot(x, y1, 'r-', linewidth=2)  # Red line
plt.title('Sine function')
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('sin(x)')
plt.grid(True)  # Add a grid
plt.axhline(y=0, color='k', linestyle='-', alpha=0.3)  # Horizontal line at y=0

# Second subplot (top-right)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 2)  # 2 rows, 2 columns, position 2
plt.plot(x, y2, 'b-', linewidth=2)  # Blue line
plt.title('Cosine function')
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('cos(x)')
plt.grid(True)
plt.axhline(y=0, color='k', linestyle='-', alpha=0.3)

# Third subplot (bottom-left)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 3)  # 2 rows, 2 columns, position 3
plt.plot(x, y3, 'g-', linewidth=2)  # Green line
plt.title('Damped sine')
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('exp(-0.2x) * sin(x)')
plt.grid(True)
plt.axhline(y=0, color='k', linestyle='-', alpha=0.3)

# Fourth subplot (bottom-right)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 4)  # 2 rows, 2 columns, position 4
plt.plot(x, y4, 'orange', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', marker='o', markevery=10, markersize=5)
plt.title('Quadratic function')
plt.xlabel('X')
plt.ylabel('x²/20')
plt.grid(True)

# Adding an annotation on the last plot
plt.annotate('Point (5, 1.25)', xy=(5, 1.25), xytext=(6, 1.8),
             arrowprops=dict(facecolor='black', shrink=0.05, width=1.5))

# Adjusting the layout - prevents overlapping
plt.tight_layout()

# Adding an overall title for the whole figure
plt.suptitle('Example of plots with subplots - procedural approach', fontsize=16)
plt.subplots_adjust(top=0.9)  # Add space for the main title

# Displaying the plot
plt.show()

PNG (Portable Network Graphics) - a raster file, a popular format for saving images on the Internet.
JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group) - a raster file, a popular format for saving photographic images.
SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) - a vector file that scales well and preserves quality across different resolutions.
PDF (Portable Document Format) - a vector document format, popular for printing and viewing documents.
EPS (Encapsulated PostScript) - a vector file, often used in scientific publications and printed materials.
TIFF (Tagged Image File Format) - a raster file, popular in professional printing and graphics.
WebP is a modern image format developed by Google that offers better compression and lower quality loss compared to the popular JPEG and PNG formats, which contributes to faster loading of web pages and savings in data transfer.

title: 31  Matplotlib - scatter plot
text:
A scatter plot is used when we want to present the relationship between two variables or the distribution of data points in a two-dimensional space. A scatter plot is suitable for both continuous and discrete data, when we want to illustrate patterns, correlation, or relationships between variables.
Here are some situations in which scatter plots are used:

Analyzing the correlation between two variables, for example the relationship between age and income.
Presenting the distribution of data points, for example showing the geographic distribution of stores in a city.
Exploring data in order to understand its structure and find patterns, groups, or anomalies, for example to identify data clusters in cluster analysis (clustering).
Detecting outliers in the data, for example for detecting unusual observations in a dataset.
Comparing different groups or categories of data, for example comparing the economic growth of different countries relative to their public debt.

Scatter plots are particularly useful when dealing with data of a different nature (continuous or discrete) and when we want to investigate correlation, groups, patterns, or outliers.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

1plt.plot([1, 2, 3, 4], [10, 20, 25, 30], color='lightblue', linewidth=3)
2plt.scatter([0.3, 3.8, 1.2, 2.5], [11, 25, 9, 26], color='darkgreen', marker='^')
3plt.xlim(0.5, 4.5)
plt.show(block=True)


1

plt.plot([1, 2, 3, 4], [10, 20, 25, 30], color='lightblue', linewidth=3) - Creates a line chart with the given point coordinates (1, 10), (2, 20), (3, 25) and (4, 30). The line color is light blue (lightblue) and its width is 3.

2

plt.scatter([0.3, 3.8, 1.2, 2.5], [11, 25, 9, 26], color='darkgreen', marker='^') - Creates a scatter plot with the given point coordinates (0.3, 11), (3.8, 25), (1.2, 9) and (2.5, 26). - The color of the points is dark green (darkgreen) and their shape is upward-filled triangles (^).

3

plt.xlim(0.5, 4.5) - Sets the range of values on the X axis, starting from 0.5 to 4.5.












import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot([1, 2, 3, 4], [10, 20, 25, 30], color='lightblue', linewidth=3)
ax.scatter([0.3, 3.8, 1.2, 2.5], [11, 25, 9, 26], color='darkgreen', marker='^')
ax.set_xlim(0.5, 4.5)
plt.show()









import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

house_prices = [230000, 350000, 480000, 280000, 420000, 610000, 390000, 580000]
square_meters = [90, 140, 210, 100, 170, 260, 150, 240]
1plt.scatter(square_meters, house_prices, color='blue', marker='o')
plt.xlabel('Area [m2]')
plt.ylabel('House price [PLN]')
plt.title('Relationship between area and house price')

1

plt.scatter(square_meters, house_prices, color='blue', marker='o'): creates a scatter plot with the house areas on the X axis (square_meters) and the house prices on the Y axis (house_prices). The points are blue (color='blue') and have a circle shape (marker='o').

house_prices = [230000, 350000, 480000, 280000, 420000, 610000, 390000, 580000]
square_meters = [90, 140, 210, 100, 170, 260, 150, 240]

from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

x = [1, -3, 4, 5, 6]
y = [2, 6, -4, 1, 2]
area = [70, 60, 1, 50, 2]
1plt.scatter(x, y, marker=">", color="brown", alpha=0.5, s=area)
plt.show(block=True)


1

The code plt.scatter(x, y, marker=">", color="brown", alpha=0.5, s=area) creates a scatter plot. x: a list or array of the x coordinates of the points on the chart. y: a list or array of the y coordinates of the points on the chart. The x and y values must have the same length in order to represent each point on the chart. marker: the symbol representing the shape of the points on the chart. In this case, we use ">" which means a right-pointing arrow. color: the color of the points on the chart. In this case, we use the color “brown”. alpha: the transparency of the points on the chart, where a value of 1 means fully opaque and 0 means fully transparent. In this case, we use a value of 0.5 which means the points are semi-transparent. s: the size of the points on the chart, which can be a single value or a list/array of values with the same length as the x and y coordinates.












import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


1data = {
    'product_id': [101, 102, 103, 104, 105, 106, 107, 108, 109, 110],
    'price': [19.99, 29.99, 14.99, 49.99, 9.99, 39.99, 24.99, 34.99, 44.99, 15.99],

}

3x_values = df['price']
4y_values = df['units_sold']
5colors = df['rating']
6sizes = df['discount'] * 1000 + 50

7plt.scatter(x_values, y_values, s=sizes, c=colors, alpha=0.7, cmap='viridis')

8plt.colorbar(label='Product rating')

9plt.xlabel('Product price (PLN)')
10plt.ylabel('Number of units sold')
11plt.title('Sales analysis')

12plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

13plt.show()


1

data = {...}: creates a dictionary containing data about products, where each key corresponds to a column name and the values are lists containing data for 10 products (product id, price, number of units sold, rating, and discount).

2

df = pd.DataFrame(data): converts the data dictionary into a pandas DataFrame, which creates an organized data table with named columns.

3

x_values = df['price']: extracts the column with product prices and assigns it to the variable x_values, which will be used as the X coordinates on the chart.

4

y_values = df['units_sold']: extracts the column with the number of units sold and assigns it to the variable y_values, which will be used as the Y coordinates on the chart.

5

colors = df['rating']: extracts the column with product ratings and assigns it to the variable colors, which will be used to assign colors to the points on the chart.

6

sizes = df['discount'] * 1000 + 50: creates the variable sizes defining the size of the points by multiplying the discount value by 1000 and adding 50, which ensures the visibility of all points (minimum size of 50 units).

7

plt.scatter(x_values, y_values, s=sizes, c=colors, alpha=0.7, cmap='viridis'): creates a scatter plot using prices as the X coordinates, sales as Y, point sizes determined by the discounts, colors based on the ratings, with a transparency of 0.7 and the ‘viridis’ color palette.

8

plt.colorbar(label='Product rating'): adds a colorbar to the chart, which shows the scale of product ratings and their corresponding colors.

9

plt.xlabel('Product price (PLN)'): sets the X axis label, describing that it shows the product price in zlotys.

10

plt.ylabel('Number of units sold'): sets the Y axis label, describing that it shows the number of units sold of the product.

11

plt.title('Sales analysis'): gives the chart the title “Sales analysis”.

12

plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3): enables the grid on the chart with a transparency of 0.3, which improves the readability of the data.

13

plt.show(): displays the prepared chart.

title: 32  Matplotlib - pie chart
text:
32.1 Donut chart
A pie chart is used when we want to present the proportions of different categories or segments relative to the whole. It is particularly useful when we have a small number of categories (usually no more than 5-7) and when the data is qualitative (categorical). A pie chart allows for a visual understanding of the percentage shares of individual categories within the entire dataset.
Examples of data for which a pie chart is used:
Although pie charts have their uses, they are also criticized for limited precision in assessing proportions. Therefore, it is often recommended to use other types of charts, such as bar charts or stacked bar charts, which can be clearer and more precise in comparing values between categories.
The pie function is used to create pie charts. It allows for a visual representation of the proportions of different segments relative to the whole.
The function syntax is plt.pie(x, explode=None, labels=None, colors=None, autopct=None, shadow=False, startangle=0, counterclock=True), where:
Switching to a color map:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

1np.random.seed(345)
2data = np.random.randint(20, 100, 6)
3total = sum(data)
4data_per = data / total * 100
5explode = (0.2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)
6plt.pie(data_per, explode=explode, labels=[round(i, 2) for i in list(data_per)])
7circle = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.7, color='white')
8p = plt.gcf()
9p.gca().add_artist(circle)
10plt.show()


1

np.random.seed(345): sets the seed of the random number generator to the value 345, which ensures the reproducibility of the results.

2

data = np.random.randint(20, 100, 6): generates an array of 6 random integers in the range from 20 to 99 (inclusive).

3

total = sum(data): calculates the sum of all the generated numbers.

4

data_per = data / total * 100: calculates the percentage values of each number relative to the total sum.

5

explode = (0.2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0): creates a tuple defining the slice offset for each element (the first slice will be offset by 0.2).

6

plt.pie(data_per, explode=explode, labels=[round(i, 2) for i in list(data_per)]): creates a pie chart with the percentage values, with the specified offset and labels rounded to 2 decimal places.

7

circle = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.7, color='white'): creates a white circle centered at the point (0, 0) with a radius of 0.7.

8

p = plt.gcf(): gets the current figure object (get current figure).

9

p.gca().add_artist(circle): adds the created white circle to the current chart axes, creating a “donut chart” effect.

10

plt.show(): displays the chart.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(345)
data = np.random.randint(20, 100, 6)
total = sum(data)
data_per = data / total * 100
explode = (0.2, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.pie(data_per, explode=explode, labels=[round(i, 2) for i in list(data_per)])
circle = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.7, color='white')
ax.add_artist(circle)
plt.show()

title: 33  Matplotlib - bar chart
text:
A bar chart is used to present categorical or discrete data. It is a commonly used type of chart that helps to visually compare values or quantities for different categories. Here are a few types of data for which a bar chart can be used:

Frequencies: A bar chart is used to present the number of occurrences of different categories, such as survey results, consumer preferences, or different population groups.
Proportions: It can be used to present the percentage share of individual categories in the whole, e.g. the market share of different companies, percentage test results, or the percentage distribution of the population by age.
Numeric values: A bar chart can present numeric values associated with different categories, e.g. product sales, revenue from different sources, or the average temperature in different cities.
Time series data: A bar chart can also be used to present time series data when changes occur at regular time intervals, e.g. annual sales, monthly rainfall, or weekly revenue.

It is worth noting that bar charts are appropriate when dealing with a small number of categories, because too many bars on a chart can make it difficult to interpret. In such cases, it is worth considering other types of charts, such as a pie chart or a proportional chart.
The bar function in the Matplotlib library is used to create bar charts. Bar charts are often used when we want to compare values of different categories.
The function syntax is plt.bar(x, height, width=0.8, bottom=None, align='center', data=None, **kwargs), where:

x - positions of the bars on the X axis. This can be a sequence of numeric values or a list of labels that will be placed on the X axis.
height - the height of the bars.
width - the width of the bars.
bottom - the position of the bottom edge of the bars. Set to None by default, which means the bars start from zero.
align - how the bars are centered along the X axis. Set to ‘center’ by default.
data - a DataFrame object that contains the data for the chart.
**kwargs - additional arguments for chart formatting, such as color, transparency, axis labels, title, and legend.


1import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

2categories = ['Category 1', 'Category 2', 'Category 3']
3values = [10, 20, 15]

4plt.bar(categories, values, color='green', alpha=0.5)

5plt.title('Bar chart')
6plt.xlabel('Categories')
7plt.ylabel('Values')

8plt.show()


1

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt: imports the matplotlib.pyplot library as plt, which is needed to create charts.

2

categories = ['Category 1', 'Category 2', 'Category 3']: creates a list categories containing the names of three categories that will be used on the X axis of the bar chart.

3

values = [10, 20, 15]: creates a list values containing the numeric values corresponding to each category - these values determine the height of the individual bars.

4

plt.bar(categories, values, color='green', alpha=0.5): creates a bar chart, where:

5

plt.title('Bar chart'): gives the chart the title “Bar chart”.

6

plt.xlabel('Categories'): adds an X axis label with the text “Categories”.

The barh function is used to create horizontal bar charts. Horizontal bar charts are often used when we want to compare values of different categories and the labels on the X axis are long or very numerous.
The function syntax is plt.barh(y, width, height=0.8, left=None, align='center', data=None, **kwargs), where:

y - positions of the bars on the Y axis. This can be a sequence of numeric values or a list of labels that will be placed on the Y axis.
width - the width of the bars.
height - the height of the bars.
left - the position of the left edge of the bars. Set to None by default, which means the bars start from zero.
align - how the bars are centered along the Y axis. Set to ‘center’ by default.
data - a DataFrame object that contains the data for the chart.
**kwargs - additional arguments for chart formatting, such as color, transparency, axis labels, title, and legend.

# Adding a title and axis labels
plt.title('Horizontal bar chart')
plt.xlabel('Values')
plt.ylabel('Categories')

# Displaying the chart
plt.show()

title: 34  Matplotlib - regression
text:
The following section discusses an example of how polynomial regression works. Other types of regression can be obtained by choosing a different command to generate it.
Regression - a statistical method that allows for examining the relationship between data quantities. The goal of multivariate regression is to quantitatively capture the relationships between many independent variables (explanatory variables, factors, predictors) and a dependent variable (criterion, explained variable, response).
Examples of multivariate regression:

The strength of concrete depends on the components used in its production. Question: In what proportion should these components be used so that the strength is greatest?
The price of an apartment depends on…. Question: how does the share of individual elements affect the market price so that it is highest?
Granting a loan depends on …. Question: how does the share of individual elements affect the decision to grant a loan or not? is this really regression?

In “scientific” terms, statistical research is generally meant to explain the relationships between different features of the studied population.
The goals of research in regression analysis:

Characterizing the relationship (among other things, its range, direction, and strength).
Determining a mathematical model that most reliably reflects the behavior of the response (in other words, finding an appropriate function that can later be used for prediction).
Determining which of the explanatory variables are important in the analysis of interdependence and ranking these variables according to the strength of their influence on the explained variable.
Comparing different models for one explained variable, i.e. comparing models that consist of different sets of explanatory variables.
Determining the interaction of explanatory variables and (in the case of a two-way relationship) determining the dependence of the explanatory variables on the explained variable.
Point estimation of the values of the regression coefficients (the direction and strength of interdependence as well as the statistical significance of the parameters introduced into the model).

The obtained results should be verified against the following criteria:

Determining a logical relationship between the variables, i.e. checking whether the obtained results do not conflict with the nature of the phenomenon.
Checking whether the cause precedes the effect in time.
Analyzing the strength of the relationship between variables, e.g. a high correlation between variables that in reality do not affect each other.
Checking whether the obtained model works in reality.
Consistency of the results.
Determining the agreement of the results with theoretical and experimental knowledge.
Considering the possibility of obtaining the studied effect as a manifestation of the action of different causes, as well as the possibility of several effects arising from one cause.

The most commonly used functions in regression analysis:

linear function \(f(x)=ax+b\),
polynomial function, e.g. quadratic \(f(x)=ax^2+bx+c\),
logarithmic function \(f(x) = \ln x\),
exponential function \(f(x)=e^{-x}\),
logistic function \(f(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}\).

Choosing a method for the interdependence of many variables:








Analysis method
Explained variable
Explanatory variables




Multiple regression
continuous
continuous (discrete also allowed)


Analysis of variance
continuous
qualitative


Analysis of covariance
continuous
qualitative (symbolic) and continuous


Poisson regression
discrete
various types


Logistic regression
binary
various types



Applications:

Econometrics: Multivariate regression can be used to analyze the impact of various factors on economic growth, such as investment, consumption, inflation, or the level of employment.
Medicine: In medical research, multivariate regression can help identify factors influencing the development of diseases, such as age, diet, lifestyle, or genetic predisposition.
Marketing: Multivariate regression can be used to analyze the impact of various product features on sales, e.g. prices, advertising, type of packaging, or competition.
Finance: Multivariate regression can be used to analyze the impact of various factors on return on investment, such as risk, interest rates, economic growth, or fiscal policy.
Engineering: In engineering, multivariate regression can help analyze the impact of various parameters on machine performance, such as temperature, pressure, or speed.
Social sciences: In the social sciences, multivariate regression can be used to analyze the impact of various factors on students’ educational outcomes, such as the parents’ level of education, income, or cultural environment.

We consider the influence of a set of \(k\) variables \(X_1, \ldots, X_k\) on the variable \(Y\). As many independent variables as possible should be introduced into the model, and it should include variables strongly correlated with the dependent variable while at the same time as weakly correlated with each other as possible.
A linear multivariate regression model:
\[Y=\beta_0 +\beta_1\cdot X_1+\beta_2\cdot X_2+\ldots+\beta_k\cdot X_k+\varepsilon.\] \(\beta_i\) - regression coefficients (model parameters) describing the influence of the \(i\)-th variable. \(\varepsilon\) - the random component.

import numpy as np

# Example data
x = np.array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
y = np.array([2.1, 2.9, 4.2, 6.1, 8.1, 9.9])

# Fitting a straight line (degree 1) with full results
coefs, stats = np.polynomial.polynomial.polyfit(x, y, deg=1, full=True)

# Displaying the results
print("Fit coefficients:", coefs)
print("\nDiagnostic information:")
print("Residuals:", stats[0])  # Sum of squared residuals
print("Rank of the system matrix:", stats[1])
print("Singular values of the system matrix:", stats[2])
print("Estimated fit error:", stats[3])

Fit coefficients: [1.51428571 1.61428571]

Diagnostic information:
Residuals: [0.87142857]
Rank of the system matrix: 2
Singular values of the system matrix: [1.35119311 0.41746518]
Estimated fit error: 1.3322676295501878e-15



import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Example data
np.random.seed(42)  # Setting the random seed for reproducibility of results
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 20)  # 20 evenly spaced points between 0 and 10
y = 3 * x + 7 + np.random.normal(0, 3, len(x))  # y data with random noise

# Fitting a straight line (linear regression)
coefs = np.polynomial.polynomial.polyfit(x, y, deg=1)  # Regression coefficients (y = a + b*x)

# Determining the regression line
x_fit = np.linspace(min(x), max(x), 100)  # Additional points for a smooth regression line
y_fit = coefs[0] + coefs[1] * x_fit

# Creating the chart
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.scatter(x, y, color='blue', label='Data points', alpha=0.7)  # Scatter plot
plt.plot(x_fit, y_fit, color='red', label=f'Regression line')  # Regression line

# Adjusting the appearance of the chart
plt.title('Linear regression using numpy.polyfit')
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('y')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.tight_layout()

# Displaying the chart
plt.show()









import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Example data
x = np.array([-10, -8, -6, -4, -2, 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10])  # x points
y = np.array([100, 88, 36, 16, 7, -3, 4, 16, 36, 52, 100])   # y points with random noise (greater dispersion)

# Fitting a second-degree curve
coefs = np.polynomial.polynomial.polyfit(x, y, deg=2)  # Regression coefficients (y = a + b*x + c*x^2)

# Determining the regression curve
x_fit = np.linspace(min(x), max(x), 100)  # Additional points for a smooth regression curve
y_fit = coefs[0] + coefs[1] * x_fit + coefs[2] * x_fit**2

# Creating the chart
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.scatter(x, y, color='blue', label='Data points', alpha=0.7)  # Scatter plot
plt.plot(x_fit, y_fit, color='red', label=f'Regression curve')  # Regression curve

# Adjusting the appearance of the chart
plt.title('Quadratic regression using numpy.polyfit')
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('y')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.tight_layout()

# Displaying the chart
plt.show()

title: 36  Matplotlib - chart options
text:
36.1 figure arguments in Matplotlib
In Matplotlib, a key role in managing chart properties is played by the figure object, which represents the “canvas” on which all graphical elements are drawn. The plt.figure() and plt.subplots() functions allow you to customize various aspects of the figure.
Arguments:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Creating a figure with dimensions of 10x5 inches
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with custom dimensions")
plt.show(block=True)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(dpi=200)  # Higher resolution
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with higher resolution")
plt.show(block=True)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(facecolor='lightblue', edgecolor='gray')  # Background and border color
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with a custom background")
plt.show(block=True)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(tight_layout=True)  # Automatic adjustment
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with automatic layout")
plt.show(block=True)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(num="My Figure")  # Figure name
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Figure with a name")
plt.show(block=True)
plt.show()

36.2 Styles
Matplotlib offers built-in styles that allow for quick and easy customization of the appearance of charts. Using the plt.style.use function, you can load a ready-made style, so that the charts gain a consistent and aesthetic appearance without the need to manually set all the parameters.
plt.style.use(style)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Using the 'ggplot' style
plt.style.use('ggplot')
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart in the ggplot style")
plt.show(block=True)








List of styles:

title: 37  Matplotlib - extras part 2.
text:
37.1 Horizontal and vertical lines
The functions axhline and axvline are used to add horizontal and vertical lines to a chart, respectively.
axhline draws a horizontal line passing through a specified value on the Y axis, regardless of the value on the X axis. The function syntax is axhline(y, xmin, xmax, **kwargs), where:
axvline draws a vertical line passing through a specified value on the X axis, regardless of the value on the Y axis. The function syntax is axvline(x, ymin, ymax, **kwargs), where:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.plot([0, 5], [0, 5], color='blue')
plt.axhline(2, color='red', linewidth=2)  # Horizontal line passing through Y=2
plt.axvline(3, color='green', linestyle='--')  # Vertical line passing through X=3, dashed line style
plt.show(block=True)
In the example above, axhline draws a red horizontal line passing through the value 2 on the Y axis, while axvline draws a green dashed vertical line passing through the value 3 on the X axis.
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(-5, 5, 0.1)
x1 = x[x < 0]
y1 = 1 / x1
plt.plot(x1, y1)
x2 = x[x > 0]
y2 = 1 / x2
plt.plot(x2, y2)
plt.ylim(-10, 10)
plt.axhline(y=0, linestyle="--")
plt.axvline(x=0, linestyle=":")
plt.show(block=True)

title: 37  Matplotlib - extras part 2.
text:
y - the value on the Y axis through which the line passes (default 0)
xmin, xmax - values in the range 0-1 specifying the start and end of the line on the X axis (default 0 and 1)
**kwargs - additional arguments, such as color, linewidth, or linestyle, used to control the appearance of the line



x - the value on the X axis through which the line passes (default 0)
ymin, ymax - values in the range 0-1 specifying the start and end of the line on the Y axis (default 0 and 1)
**kwargs - additional arguments, such as color, linewidth, or linestyle, used to control the appearance of the line

title: 37  Matplotlib - extras part 2.
text:
37.2 Annotations (text) on a chart
The annotate function is used to add annotations (text and arrows) to a chart in order to highlight or mark specific points or areas.
The function syntax is annotate(text, xy, xytext, arrowprops, **kwargs), where:

text - a string representing the annotation text.
xy - a tuple (x, y) specifying the coordinates of the point referred to in the annotation.
xytext - a tuple (x, y) specifying the coordinates at which the annotation text should begin. If not provided, the text will be displayed directly at the xy coordinates.
arrowprops - a dictionary containing options for drawing the arrow, such as arrowstyle, color, or linewidth. If not provided, the arrow will not be drawn.
**kwargs - additional arguments for text formatting, such as fontsize, color, or fontweight.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.plot([1, 2, 3, 4], [2, 4, 9, 16], marker='o', linestyle='-', color='blue')
plt.xlabel('X-axis')
plt.ylabel('Y-axis')

plt.annotate('Important point (2, 4)',
             xy=(2, 4),                           # Coordinates of the point to mark
             xytext=(2.5, 6),                     # Coordinates of the start of the text
             arrowprops=dict(facecolor='red'),    # Arrow properties (color)
             fontsize=12,                         # Font size
             color='blue',                        # Text color
             fontweight='bold')                   # Font weight

plt.show(block=True)








If you want to add an annotation with text only, the function syntax is annotate(text, xy, **kwargs), where:

text - a string representing the annotation text.
xy - a tuple (x, y) specifying the coordinates at which the annotation text should begin.
**kwargs - additional arguments for text formatting, such as fontsize, color, fontweight, or horizontalalignment.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.plot([1, 2, 3, 4], [2, 4, 9, 16], marker='o', linestyle='-', color='blue')
plt.xlabel('X-axis')
plt.ylabel('Y-axis')

plt.annotate('Important point (2, 4)',
             xy=(2, 4),                           # Coordinates of the start of the text
             fontsize=12,                         # Font size
             color='blue',                        # Text color
             fontweight='bold',                   # Font weight
             horizontalalignment='right')         # Right-align the text

plt.show(block=True)

title: 37  Matplotlib - extras part 2.
text:
37.3 Axis labels
The functions xlabel and ylabel are used to add labels to the X and Y axes on a chart, respectively. Axis labels help to better understand the presented data by indicating what values are represented on each axis.
The function syntax is xlabel(label, **kwargs) or ylabel(label, **kwargs), where:

label - a string representing the axis label text.
**kwargs - additional arguments for label formatting, such as fontsize, color, fontweight, or horizontalalignment.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [1, 2, 3, 4]
y = [2, 4, 6, 8]

plt.plot(x, y, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='blue')

plt.xlabel('X axis', fontsize=12, color='red', fontweight='bold')
plt.ylabel('Y axis', fontsize=12, color='green', fontweight='bold')

plt.show(block=True)








The annotate function allows the use of LaTeX syntax in the annotation text, which is especially useful when we want to add mathematical equations or symbols to a chart. To use LaTeX syntax, the text must be placed between dollar signs ($).

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)
y = np.sin(x)

plt.plot(x, y)
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('sin(x)')

# Annotation with text in LaTeX syntax
plt.annotate(r'$\sin(\frac{2\pi}{3})=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$',
             xy=(2 * np.pi / 3, np.sqrt(3) / 2),  # Coordinates of the point to mark
             xytext=(3, 1.0),                     # Coordinates of the start of the text
             fontsize=12,                         # Font size
             arrowprops=dict(facecolor='red'),    # Arrow properties (color)
             bbox=dict(facecolor='white'))        # Box around the text (background color)

plt.show(block=True)

title: 37  Matplotlib - extras part 2.
text:
37.4 Axis tick labels
The functions xticks and yticks are used to manipulate the labels of the X and Y axes and the values on the axis, respectively. They allow you to control the display of labels, the spacing between them, and the formatting.
xticks manipulates the labels and values on the X axis, and yticks on the Y axis. The function syntax is xticks(ticks, labels, **kwargs) or yticks(ticks, labels, **kwargs), where:

ticks - a list of values for which labels are to be placed on the axis. If not provided, the current values remain.
labels - a list of strings to be used as labels for the values from the ticks list. If not provided, the default labels will be used.
**kwargs - additional arguments for label formatting, such as fontsize, color, fontweight, or rotation.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.arange(0, 10, 0.1)
y = np.sin(x)

plt.plot(x, y)

xtick_vals = [0, np.pi, 2 * np.pi, 3 * np.pi]
xtick_labels = ['0', r'$\pi$', r'$2\pi$', r'$3\pi$']

ytick_vals = [-1, -0.5, 0, 0.5, 1]
ytick_labels = ['-1', '-1/2', '0', '1/2', '1']

plt.xticks(xtick_vals, xtick_labels, fontsize=12, color='red')
plt.yticks(ytick_vals, ytick_labels, fontsize=12, rotation=45)

plt.show(block=True)









import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = np.arange(-3, 3, 0.1)
y = x ** 2 + 2 * x
plt.plot(x, y)
plt.annotate(xy=(-1, 5), text="something")
plt.xticks([-2, 1, 2], color="red")
plt.ylabel("abc", color="green")
plt.show(block=True)

title: 37  Matplotlib - extras part 2.
text:
37.5 Grid
The grid function allows you to add a grid to a chart, which makes it easier to assess values on the axes and compare them. You can control the color, width, and style of the grid lines, as well as specify which axes should have a grid.
The function syntax is plt.grid(b=None, which='major', axis='both', **kwargs), where:

b - a boolean value (True/False) that determines whether the grid is to be displayed. Set to None by default, which means that Matplotlib automatically determines whether the grid should be displayed based on the configuration.
which - specifies which grid lines are to be displayed: ‘major’ (only grid lines for major ticks), ‘minor’ (grid lines for minor ticks), or ‘both’ (default - grid lines for both types of ticks).
axis - specifies which axes should have a grid: ‘both’ (both axes), ‘x’ (only the X axis), or ‘y’ (only the Y axis).
**kwargs - additional arguments for grid formatting.


import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)
y = np.sin(x)

# Creating the chart
plt.plot(x, y, color='blue', linewidth=2)

# Adding the grid
plt.grid(True, which='both', color='gray', linewidth=0.5, linestyle='--')

# Adding a title and axis labels
plt.title('Plot of the function sin(x)')
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('sin(x)')

# Displaying the chart
plt.show(block=True)









import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

x = np.linspace(0, np.pi * 2, 100)
plt.subplot(3, 1, 1)
plt.plot(x, np.sin(x), 'r')
plt.grid(True)
plt.xlim(0, np.pi * 2)
plt.subplot(3, 1, 2)
plt.plot(x, np.cos(x), 'g')
plt.grid(True)
plt.xlim(0, np.pi * 2)
plt.subplot(3, 1, 3)
plt.plot(x, np.sin(x), 'r', x, np.cos(x), 'g')
plt.grid(True)
plt.xlim(0, np.pi * 2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("fig3.png", dpi=72)
plt.show(block=True)

title: 38  Matplotlib - other charts
text:
38.1 Dual-axis chart
The twinx function in the Matplotlib library allows you to create a second Y axis that will share the X axis with the first Y axis. Thanks to this, you can easily present two data series that are measured in different units but have a common independent variable.
The function syntax is twinx(ax=None, **kwargs), where:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots()
x = np.arange(0.01, 10.0, 0.01)
y = x ** 2
ax1.plot(x, y, 'r')
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
y2 = np.sin(x)
ax2.plot(x, y2)
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show(block=True)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots()
t = np.arange(0.01, 10.0, 0.01)
s1 = np.exp(t)
ax1.plot(t, s1, 'b-')
ax1.set_xlabel('time (s)')

ax1.set_ylabel('exp', color='b')
ax1.tick_params('y', colors='b')

ax2 = ax1.twinx()
s2 = np.sin(2 * np.pi * t)
ax2.plot(t, s2, 'r.')
ax2.set_ylabel('sin', color='r')
ax2.tick_params('y', colors='r')

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show(block=True)

title: 38  Matplotlib - other charts
text:
ax - the Axes object to be used to create the new Y axis. Set to None by default, which means a new Y axis will be created.
**kwargs - additional arguments for formatting the new Y axis.

title: 38  Matplotlib - other charts
text:
38.2 Box plot
A box plot is used to present information about the distribution of numeric data and to identify outliers. It is particularly useful when analyzing continuous data that have different values and distributions. Here are a few types of data for which a box plot can be used:

Comparing groups: A box plot is used to compare the distribution of data between different groups. For example, it can be used to compare the test results of students from different schools, salaries in different sectors, or the sales values of different products.
Identifying outliers: A box plot is used to identify outliers in the data, which may indicate measurement errors, unusual observations, or extreme values. For example, it can be used to detect anomalies in meteorological data, stock market values, or medical data.
Distribution analysis: A box plot helps to understand the distribution of data, such as the median, quartiles, range of values, and potential outliers. It can be used in the analysis of data such as grades, population growth, stock value, or real estate prices.
Visualization of multidimensional data: A box plot can be used to visualize multidimensional data, presenting the distribution of many variables on a single chart. For example, you can compare variables such as age, earnings, and education in a demographic study.

It is worth noting that a box plot is particularly useful when we want to understand the distribution of data, but it does not show the specific number of observations or the values of individual data points. In such cases, other types of charts, such as a scatter plot, may be more appropriate.
A box plot shows five descriptive statistics of the data: the minimum, the first quartile (Q1), the median, the third quartile (Q3), and the maximum.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Example data
data = np.random.rand(100)

# Creating the chart
fig, ax = plt.subplots()

# Drawing the boxplot
ax.boxplot(data)

# Adding labels
ax.set_title('Boxplot')
ax.set_ylabel('Values')
ax.set_xticklabels(['Example data'])

# Displaying the chart
plt.show(block=True)










import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Creating dataset
np.random.seed(10)
data = np.random.normal(100, 20, 200)

# Creating plot
plt.boxplot(data)

# show plot
plt.show(block=True)








patch_artist=True,    # colored boxes – enables the use of “Patch” objects to draw the boxes themselves, so that we can fill them with color (further controlled by boxprops).
notch=True,           # notches at the median – draws a notch around the median line, which helps to visually assess the significance of differences between medians.
boxprops=dict(facecolor='lightblue', edgecolor='navy'), – styling of the boxes themselves:

facecolor='lightblue' – fills the box with a light blue color,
edgecolor='navy' – outlines the box in dark navy.

medianprops=dict(color='darkred'), – properties of the median line: only the dark red color (darkred).
whiskerprops=dict(color='navy'), – style of the whiskers: the “navy” color for the lines that connect the box with the ends of the data range (without taking outliers into account).
capprops=dict(color='navy'), – style of the “caps” at the ends of the whiskers: also the navy color.
flierprops=dict( – style of the outlier points (“fliers”, values beyond the reach of the whiskers):
* `marker='o'` – a circle as the marker,
* `markerfacecolor='gray'` – gray fill of the circle,
* `markersize=5` – the size of the marker,
* `linestyle='none'` – no line connecting these points.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Preparing the data
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'Mode of transport': [
        'Car', 'Car', 'Car', 'Car', 'Car',
        'Bicycle',    'Bicycle',    'Bicycle',    'Bicycle',    'Bicycle',
        'Public transport', 'Public transport', 'Public transport', 'Public transport', 'Public transport'
    ],
    'Commute time (minutes)': [
        25, 30, 28, 55, 32,
        20, 18, 22, 19, 21,
        40, 45, 42, 38, 44
    ]
})

# Preparing the data for the box plot
groups = data['Mode of transport'].unique()
times_list = [data.loc[data['Mode of transport'] == s, 'Commute time (minutes)']
              for s in groups]

# Drawing the chart
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.boxplot(
    times_list,
    tick_labels=groups,
    patch_artist=True,    # colored boxes
    notch=True,           # notches at the median
    boxprops=dict(facecolor='lightblue', edgecolor='navy'),
    medianprops=dict(color='darkred'),
    whiskerprops=dict(color='navy'),
    capprops=dict(color='navy'),
    flierprops=dict(marker='o', markerfacecolor='gray', markersize=5, linestyle='none')
)

# Axis labels and title
plt.ylabel('Commute time [minutes]', fontsize=12)
plt.title('Comparison of commute times to work\nby mode of transport', fontsize=14)

# Grid and aesthetics
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.tight_layout()

# Displaying
plt.show()

title: 38  Matplotlib - other charts
text:
38.3 Histogram
A histogram is used to present the distribution of numeric data, both continuous and discrete. A histogram shows the frequency of occurrence of data in specific intervals (bins), which allows for the analysis of the distribution and the identification of patterns. Here are a few types of data for which a histogram can be used:

Distribution analysis: A histogram can be used to analyze the distribution of data, such as grades, prices, stock values, population growth, or meteorological data. This allows you to understand how the data is distributed, whether it is concentrated around certain values, or whether it has a long tail (i.e. whether there are outliers).
Identifying trends: A histogram can help identify trends or patterns in data. For example, you can use a histogram to identify seasonal sales patterns, changes in stock market values, or population migration patterns.
Comparing groups: A histogram can also be used to compare the distribution of data between different groups. For example, it can be used to compare the test results of students from different schools, salaries in different sectors, or the sales values of different products.
Estimating parameters: A histogram can help estimate distribution parameters, such as the mean, median, or variance, which can be useful in statistical analysis.

It is worth noting that a histogram is suitable for numeric data, but it is not intended for presenting categorical data. In such cases, other types of charts, such as a bar chart, may be more appropriate.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10,
     10, 11, 11, 13, 13, 15, 16, 17, 18, 18]

plt.hist(x, bins=4)
plt.show(block=True)









import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

ages = [20, 22, 25, 27, 21, 23, 37, 31, 61, 45, 41, 32]
bins = [18, 25, 35, 60, 100]
cats2 = pd.cut(ages, [18, 26, 36, 61, 100], right=False)
print(cats2)
group_names = ['Youth', 'YoungAdult',
               'MiddleAged', 'Senior']
data = pd.cut(ages, bins, labels=group_names)
plt.hist(data)
plt.show(block=True)

[[18, 26), [18, 26), [18, 26), [26, 36), [18, 26), ..., [26, 36), [61, 100), [36, 61), [36, 61), [26, 36)]
Length: 12
Categories (4, interval[int64, left]): [[18, 26) < [26, 36) < [36, 61) < [61, 100)]










import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

ages = [20, 22, 25, 27, 21, 23, 37, 31, 61, 45, 41, 32]
bins = [18, 25, 35, 60, 100]
plt.hist(ages, bins=bins)
plt.show(block=True)









import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10,
     10, 11, 11, 13, 13, 15, 14, 12, 18, 18]

plt.hist(x, bins=[0, 5, 10, 15, 20])
plt.xticks([0, 5, 10, 15, 20])
plt.show(block=True)









import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10,
     10, 11, 11, 13, 13, 15, 14, 12, 18, 18]

plt.hist(x, bins=[0, 5, 10, 15, 20], cumulative=True)
plt.xticks([0, 5, 10, 15, 20])
plt.show(block=True)









import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

x = [1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10,
     10, 11, 11, 13, 13, 15, 14, 12, 18, 18]

plt.hist(x, bins=[0, 5, 10, 15, 20], density=True)
plt.xticks([0, 5, 10, 15, 20])
plt.show(block=True)









import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 1. We create a data frame
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'Day': list(range(1, 17)),
    'Commute time (min)': [25, 30, 20, 35, 40, 22, 28, 33,
                           27, 32, 29, 31, 26, 24, 34, 36]
})

# 2. We draw the histogram
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

plt.hist(
    data['Commute time (min)'],
    bins=8,                    # number of bars
    edgecolor='black',         # bar borders
    alpha=0.7                  # transparency for better aesthetics
)

# 3. Labels and aesthetics
plt.title('Histogram of commute time to work')
plt.xlabel('Commute time (minutes)')
plt.ylabel('Number of days')
plt.xticks(range(20, 45, 5))  # adjusted ticks on the X axis
plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.75)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# 1. Data frame
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'Day': list(range(1, 15)),
    'Number of coffees per day': [1, 2, 3, 3, 2, 4, 5, 3, 2, 1, 4, 3, 2, 3]
})

# 2. We draw the histogram with new arguments:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))

plt.hist(
    data['Number of coffees per day'],
    bins=[0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 3.5, 4.5, 5.5],  # precise intervals around values 1-5
    color='lightblue',                     # bar color
    edgecolor='black',                    # border of each bar
    alpha=0.7                             # transparency
)

# 3. Labels and aesthetics
plt.title('Histogram: number of coffees drunk per day')
plt.xlabel('Number of coffees')
plt.ylabel('Number of days')
plt.xticks(range(1, 6))                  # from 1 to 5 coffees
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: 38  Matplotlib - other charts
text:
38.4 3D plots

38.4.1 Helix
\[\begin{cases}
x=a\cos (t) \\
y=a\sin(t) \\
z=at
\end{cases}\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
ax = plt.axes(projection='3d')
t = np.linspace(0, 15, 1000)
a = 3
xline = a * np.sin(t)
yline = a * np.cos(t)
zline = a * t
ax.plot3D(xline, yline, zline)
plt.show(block=True)










38.4.2 Torus
\[p(\alpha,\ \beta)=\Big((R+r\cos \alpha)\cos \beta,\ (R+r\cos \alpha)\sin \beta,\ r\sin \alpha\Big)\]

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
ax = plt.axes(projection='3d')
r = 1
R = 5
alpha = np.arange(0, 2 * np.pi, 0.1)
beta = np.arange(0, 2 * np.pi, 0.1)
alpha, beta = np.meshgrid(alpha, beta)
x = (R + r * np.cos(alpha)) * np.cos(beta)
y = (R + r * np.cos(alpha)) * np.sin(beta)
z = r * np.sin(alpha)
ax.plot_wireframe(x, y, z)
plt.show(block=True)

title: 39  Matplotlib - heatmap
text:
A heatmap is a two-dimensional visualization in which numeric values are represented using colors. The intensity of the color corresponds to the magnitude (value) at a specific point on the grid.
Main applications of heatmaps

Correlation matrices

They allow you to quickly identify strong and weak relationships between variables in a dataset.

They facilitate the preliminary selection of features, e.g. in predictive modeling.

Spatio-temporal analysis

Visualization of the intensity of events (e.g. sales, network traffic) along the time axis versus categories or zones.

It helps to detect seasonal, daily, or weekly patterns.

Geographic maps

Presentation of the density of events or values (e.g. illnesses, accidents) across geographic areas.

They enable the quick identification of “hotspots”.

User behavior analysis

Tracking clicks, mouse movement, or scrolling on websites/applications.

Optimization of the layout of interface elements for usability.

Resource and performance monitoring

Visualization of server load, memory usage, or production output in real time.

It helps to catch anomalies or overloads.

Detecting anomalies and deviations

Identification of unusual readings, e.g. in medical, financial, or network data.

Quick indication of areas requiring further analysis.



import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = {
    'Day': ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday'] * 3,
    'Time of day': ['Morning']*5 + ['Afternoon']*5 + ['Evening']*5,
    'Number of customers': [50, 60, 55, 65, 80,    # Morning
                            120, 130, 125, 140, 160,  # Afternoon
                            90, 100, 95, 110, 120]    # Evening
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)


heatmap_data = df.pivot(index='Day', columns='Time of day', values='Number of customers')

plt.imshow(heatmap_data,
           cmap='YlOrRd',      # palette from yellow (low) to red (high)
           aspect='auto')      # automatic aspect ratio adjustment

cbar = plt.colorbar()
cbar.set_label('Number of customers', rotation=270, labelpad=15)

plt.xticks(ticks=range(len(heatmap_data.columns)),
           labels=heatmap_data.columns,
           rotation=45,
           ha='right')
plt.yticks(ticks=range(len(heatmap_data.index)),
           labels=heatmap_data.index)

plt.xlabel('Time of day')
plt.ylabel('Day of the week')
plt.title('Average number of customers in the store')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = {
    'Day': ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday'] * 3,
    'Time of day': ['Morning']*5 + ['Afternoon']*5 + ['Evening']*5,
    'Number of customers': [50, 60, 55, 65, 80,    # Morning
                            120, 130, 125, 140, 160,  # Afternoon
                            90, 100, 95, 110, 120]    # Evening
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

heatmap_data = df.pivot(index='Day', columns='Time of day', values='Number of customers')

fig, ax = plt.subplots()

im = ax.imshow(
    heatmap_data,
    cmap='YlOrRd',      # palette from yellow (low) to red (high)
    aspect='auto'       # automatic aspect ratio adjustment
)

cbar = fig.colorbar(im, ax=ax)
cbar.set_label('Number of customers', rotation=270, labelpad=15)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(heatmap_data.columns)))
ax.set_xticklabels(heatmap_data.columns, rotation=45, ha='right')
ax.set_yticks(range(len(heatmap_data.index)))
ax.set_yticklabels(heatmap_data.index)

ax.set_xlabel('Time of day')
ax.set_ylabel('Day of the week')
ax.set_title('Average number of customers in the store')

fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: 40  Matplotlib - area chart
text:
40.1 Main applications of area charts
An area chart is a type of visualization in which successive data series are presented as stacked areas/layers. The height of each layer corresponds to the value of a given category, and the sum of the heights of all layers shows the total value.
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

data = {
    'Month': ['January', 'February', 'March', 'April', 'May'],
    'Sales': [1000, 1200, 900, 1100, 1300],
    'Services': [200, 250, 230, 260, 300],
    'Advertising': [150, 180, 170, 160, 190]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

x = df['Month']
y1 = df['Sales']
y2 = df['Services']
y3 = df['Advertising']

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
plt.stackplot(x, y1, y2, y3, labels=['Sales', 'Services', 'Advertising'])
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.title('Company revenue broken down by source')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Amount (PLN)')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

data = {

title: 40  Matplotlib - area chart
text:
Analysis of total composition over time

It allows you to see how individual components (e.g. revenue sources, expenses, market segments) contribute to the total value at successive moments.

Useful for monitoring percentage shares and changes in proportions.

Comparison of multidimensional trends

It enables the simultaneous tracking of many time series (“layers”) in a single space, which makes it easier to detect mutual correlations and turning points.

Ideal for presenting multichannel data (e.g. website traffic from different sources).

Presentation of market shares or segments

It shows how the shares of individual products, regions, or customer groups change over time.

It makes it easier to identify “growing” and “shrinking” segments.

Budgeting and accounting

It visualizes the structure of costs or revenue by category over the course of the budget cycle.

It helps to quickly catch excessive expenses or sources of revenue growth.

Monitoring resources and workloads

Tracking the use of resources (e.g. staff, machines) across different projects or departments.

It enables planning and balancing the workload.

Comparison of scenarios or variants

Overlaying forecasted data alongside historical data makes it easier to compare the “expected” vs. the “actual” behavior of indicators.

It supports strategic decisions by illustrating deviations.

title: 41  Seaborn - basics
text:
41.3 Line plot
The sns.lineplot() function in the Seaborn library is used to create line plots, which allow for the visualization of the relationship between two variables, especially in the case of time data. A line plot is useful for presenting trends and changes in data over time or another continuous variable.
Here is an example of using the sns.lineplot() function:

import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Example data
data = {
    'year': [2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019],
    'value': [100, 110, 105, 120, 130, 140, 150, 170, 180, 200]
}

# Loading the data as a Pandas DataFrame
import pandas as pd
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Creating the line plot
sns.lineplot(x='year', y='value', data=df)

# Adding a title and axis labels
plt.title('Line plot example')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Value')

# Displaying the chart
plt.show()








In the example above, we create a line plot based on the DataFrame df. The x and y parameters specify the variables that are to be presented on the chart axes. The data parameter indicates the data source, in this case a Pandas DataFrame.
The sns.lineplot() function offers additional parameters that allow for modifying the chart. Examples include:

The hue parameter: allows you to add coloring of the lines based on the values of a categorical variable.


import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Example data
data = {
    'year': [2010, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019] * 2,
    'value': [100, 110, 105, 120, 130, 140, 150, 170, 180, 200] +
             [200, 180, 170, 160, 150, 140, 130, 120, 110, 100],
    'category': ['A'] * 10 + ['B'] * 10
}

# Loading the data as a Pandas DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Creating a line plot with the 'hue' parameter
sns.lineplot(x='year', y='value', hue='category', data=df)

# Adding a title and axis labels
plt.title('Line plot example with the hue parameter')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Value')

# Displaying the chart
plt.show()









The style parameter: allows you to change the style of the line based on the values of a categorical variable.

sns.lineplot(x='year', y='value', style='category', data=df)

The markers parameter: allows you to add markers to the data points.

sns.lineplot(x='year', y='value', markers=True, data=df)

The ci parameter: allows you to add a confidence interval to the chart, which can be useful in the case of multiple measurements for the same values on the X axis.

sns.lineplot(x='year', y='value', ci=95, data=df)

text:
41.5 Bar chart

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Example DataFrame
data = {'Category': ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'A', 'B', 'C', 'D'],
        'Value': [10, 15, 8, 12, 11, 14, 9, 13],
        'Group': ['X', 'X', 'X', 'X', 'Y', 'Y', 'Y', 'Y']}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

Basic bar chart:

sns.barplot(x='Category', y='Value', data=df)
plt.title('Basic bar chart')
plt.show()









Example with estimator:


sns.barplot(x='Category', y='Value', data=df, estimator=max)
plt.title("Bar chart with 'estimator=max'")
plt.show()








In this example, estimator=max causes the height of the bars to be the maximum value for each category.

Example with errorbar:


sns.barplot(x='Category', y='Value', data=df, errorbar=None)
plt.title("Bar chart with 'errorbar=None'")
plt.show()








Here, errorbar=None causes the confidence intervals not to be displayed on the chart.

Example with palette:


sns.barplot(x='Category', y='Value', data=df, palette='Greens', hue='Category')
plt.title("Bar chart with 'palette=Greens'")
plt.show()








The palette='Greens' argument sets the color palette to shades of green.

Example with hue:


sns.barplot(x='Category', y='Value', hue='Group', data=df)
plt.title("Bar chart with 'hue=Group'")
plt.show()








In this example, the hue='Group' argument splits the bars for each category according to the values in the ‘Group’ column.

Example with orient:


sns.barplot(x='Value', y='Category', data=df, orient='h')
plt.title("Bar chart with 'orient=h'")
plt.show()








Here, orient='h' changes the orientation of the chart to horizontal.

Example with order:


sns.barplot(x='Category', y='Value', data=df, order=['B', 'D', 'A', 'C'])
plt.title("Bar chart with 'order=['B', 'D', 'A', 'C']'")
plt.show()








The order=['B', 'D', 'A', 'C'] argument specifies the order of the categories on the x axis.

title: 41  Seaborn - basics
text:
41.6 Styles
The Seaborn library allows you to customize the aesthetics of charts using color palettes and styles, which lets you obtain attractive and readable data visualizations. Here are a few examples of how we can customize the aesthetics of charts in Seaborn:

Color palettes:

Color palettes allow you to change the coloring of charts. Seaborn offers several built-in color palettes that can be easily applied to charts.
Example of using the ‘coolwarm’ color palette in a scatter plot:

import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

iris_data = sns.load_dataset('iris')

# Setting the 'coolwarm' color palette
sns.set_palette('coolwarm')

sns.scatterplot(x='sepal_length', y='sepal_width', hue='species', data=iris_data)

plt.show()








To reset the color palette to the default, use sns.set_palette('deep').

Chart styles:

Seaborn allows you to change the style of charts, which can affect the overall appearance and readability of the visualization. The library offers several built-in styles.
Example of using the ‘whitegrid’ style in a scatter plot:

import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

iris_data = sns.load_dataset('iris')

# Setting the 'whitegrid' style
sns.set_style('whitegrid')

sns.scatterplot(x='sepal_length', y='sepal_width', hue='species', data=iris_data)

plt.show()








Other available styles are: ‘darkgrid’, ‘white’, ‘ticks’, and ‘dark’. To reset the style to the default, use sns.set_style('darkgrid').

Scaling of graphical elements:

Seaborn allows you to scale graphical elements, which can be useful in the case of different screen resolutions or the need to change the size of charts.
Example of using the ‘poster’ context in a scatter plot:

import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

iris_data = sns.load_dataset('iris')

# Setting the 'poster' context
sns.set_context('poster')

sns.scatterplot(x='sepal_length', y='sepal_width', hue='species', data=iris_data)

plt.show()








Other available contexts are: ‘paper’, ‘notebook’, and ‘talk’. To reset the context to the default, use sns.set_context('notebook').
By combining these methods, you can create varied, attractive, and readable data visualizations.
